body: I can't find which code is responsible for this right now (https://github.com/algolia/instantsearch/blob/999d3ccf1c49b61f6c49bad17c92fef0e635e209/packages/react-instantsearch/src/widgets/SearchBox.tsx#L89-L93 for react), but if I remember it correctly, this is done on purpose to prevent another issue. What's odd is that these other methods of navigating the browser don't cause the input to blur. Does this also happen in multi-page apps or only because there's a history pop state handler?

I have had a lot of trouble pinning down the exact cause and pattern. Until I accidentally noticed it was not happening with codesandbox.io and realised it was the focus.

My actual app is much more complicated but is basically an overlay over a multi-page website and while using the search, the InstantSearch router is used to update the url without reloading the page. Reloading the page reopens the overlay and applies the search state from the url (basic InstantSearch router behaviour). When that happens, the input has the correct value again.
When one of those real page loads is in the browser history, navigating to it will apply the value to the field correctly. So the initial `read()` of the router seems to ignore the focus in the field.
This issue only occurs when the history api updates the url without a full new page load.

I hope that answers your question about multi-page. All of this can be demonstrated with the html I included in the ticket.

> What's odd is that these other methods of navigating the browser don't cause the input to blur.

I actually prefer that. It allows quick navigation between results and be ready to type a search term. But if I have to choose between losing focus and losing the searchterm, I'll settle for keeping the searchterm and not losing my history. I think my client would make the same decision (I'll have to check tomorrow)
